original_hypothesis.title: LiquidityGauge share token may become undercollateralized if underlying token is fee-on-transfer or otherwise non-1:1, breaking withdrawals for later users
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge mints gauge tokens equal to the requested deposit amount, not the actual amount received. For fee-on-transfer (or deflationary) tokens, the gauge receives less underlying than minted shares, violating 1:1 backing and causing eventual withdrawal failures/shortfalls.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a classic practical integration bug if governance mistakenly whitelists a non-standard token (or token upgrades into such behavior). The protocol explicitly assumes standard ERC20s, but in production this misconfiguration is common and leads to direct loss/failure on withdrawals.
deployment_assumptions: A whitelisted market's underlying token (used to deploy LiquidityGauge) charges transfer fees, rebases negatively, or otherwise results in received < sent on transferFrom
Or the underlying token's behavior changes via upgrade after gauge deployment
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No on-chain allowlist/validation of ERC20 semantics in LendingLedger.whiteListLendingMarket; LiquidityGauge.depositUnderlying mints _amount regardless of received amount.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mints based on requested amount, not actual received underlying

preconditions: Underlying token does not transfer exact _amount to the gauge (fee-on-transfer/deflation/rebase/blacklist)
Users are able to deposit and later withdraw
No compensating logic exists to mint based on balance delta

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no balanceBefore/balanceAfter accounting; no token vetting

analysis_questions: Are there any guards in LendingLedger.whitelist flow preventing non-standard tokens? (none visible)
Could an upgraded underlying token later introduce fees, bricking the gauge?
validation_requirements: Simulate with a fee-on-transfer ERC20: deposit 100, receive 99, mint 100
Attempt withdrawals after multiple deposits; confirm insolvency revert/shortfall

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits into a gauge backed by fee-on-transfer token. Shares minted exceed backing; attacker may withdraw early while later users revert or receive less, effectively shifting losses.
prerequisites: Non-standard underlying token is used or becomes non-standard

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Direct principal loss/withdrawal failures for gauge users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All LiquidityGauge depositors for that market

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for non-standard ERC20 transfer semantics

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare totalSupply vs underlying balance on-chain

validation_result.impact_summary: LiquidityGauge mints shares based on requested amount, not actual received amount. If underlying token charges transfer fees, gauge becomes undercollateralized, causing withdrawal failures for later users.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The depositUnderlying function in LiquidityGauge.sol mints _amount gauge tokens after calling safeTransferFrom for _amount underlying tokens. For fee-on-transfer (or deflationary) ERC20 tokens, the actual amount received by the gauge contract is less than _amount, but the full _amount of gauge tokens is minted. This creates a systemic deficit: totalSupply() of gauge tokens exceeds IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)). Over time, as users deposit and withdraw, the deficit compounds. The last users to call withdrawUnderlying will face reverts because the gauge contract lacks sufficient underlying tokens to honor the withdrawal, despite holding valid gauge tokens.

The withdrawUnderlying function similarly uses the raw _amount for the safeTransfer back to the user, meaning it attempts to send more underlying than the gauge received for that share. This is a classic integration bug when dealing with non-standard ERC20 tokens.

There is no validation in LendingLedger.whiteListLendingMarket or anywhere else in the codebase that checks whether the underlying token has standard transfer semantics. The governance address has full discretion over which tokens are whitelisted, and tokens can also be upgraded to introduce fee-on-transfer behavior after gauge deployment.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof-of-signal is accurate. LiquidityGauge.sol depositUnderlying (lines ~32-37) performs safeTransferFrom followed by _mint with the same _amount parameter, with no balance delta check. withdrawUnderlying (lines ~40-45) performs _burn then safeTransfer with the same _amount, which will fail if the gauge is undercollateralized. The whiteListLendingMarket function in LendingLedger.sol (lines ~131-145) deploys a LiquidityGauge with the market address as underlying token but performs no validation of the token's transfer semantics.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Mints _amount gauge tokens without verifying the actual amount of underlying tokens received. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the received amount is less than _amount, causing the gauge to become undercollateralized. Should use balanceBefore/balanceAfter pattern to determine actual received amount.

Attempts to transfer _amount underlying tokens back to the user. If the gauge is undercollateralized due to fee-on-transfer deposits, this safeTransfer will revert for later withdrawers, permanently locking their funds.

No validation of the underlying token's ERC20 semantics (fee-on-transfer, rebasing, etc.) is performed when whitelisting a market and deploying a LiquidityGauge. Any ERC20 address is accepted unconditionally.

calling_sequence: 2. IERC20(underlyingToken).safeTransferFrom(user, gauge, 100) executes - fee-on-transfer token delivers only 99 tokens to gauge
3. _mint(user, 100) mints 100 gauge tokens to user (1 token more than underlying received)
5. Gauge now has 99 underlying tokens but 100 gauge tokens in totalSupply
6. Repeat: after N deposits, deficit grows to N tokens
7. Later user calls withdrawUnderlying(100) - _burn succeeds but safeTransfer(user, 100) reverts if gauge balance < 100
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is triggered at LiquidityGauge.sol:35 where _mint(_user, _amount) uses the requested amount rather than the actually received amount. The fix requires computing the balance delta: balanceBefore = IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)); then after transfer, received = balanceOf - balanceBefore; _mint(_user, received). Without this, each deposit with a fee-on-transfer token increases the deficit between totalSupply and actual underlying balance.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A whitelisted market's underlying token charges transfer fees or results in received < sent on transferFrom
Or the underlying token's behavior changes via upgrade after gauge deployment
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.sol only deploys VotingEscrow, GaugeController, and LendingLedger. No markets are whitelisted and no gauges are deployed in the script. The choice of underlying token for LiquidityGauge instances is entirely a post-deployment governance decision with no on-chain validation of token semantics.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid. If governance whitelists a market backed by a fee-on-transfer token (or a token that later upgrades to include transfer fees): (1) Users deposit via depositUnderlying, receiving more gauge tokens than underlying delivered. (2) The deficit grows with each deposit. (3) An attacker who is aware of the deficit can withdraw early (first-mover advantage), receiving full underlying for their shares. (4) Later withdrawers face reverts when the gauge's underlying balance is insufficient. This results in direct principal loss for the last users to withdraw. The attack requires no special technical sophistication - simply being aware of the deficit and withdrawing before others.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If a fee-on-transfer token is used as underlying: (1) Direct principal loss for later withdrawers who cannot withdraw their full deposit. (2) The deficit grows linearly with cumulative deposit volume. (3) LendingLedger reward accounting also becomes incorrect since sync_ledger records inflated amounts. (4) No recovery mechanism exists in the contracts - stuck funds cannot be rescued without deploying a new contract. Severity is HIGH conditional on token choice, but the code defect exists regardless.
recommended_mitigations: Use the balance-before/balance-after pattern in depositUnderlying: uint256 balBefore = IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)); IERC20(underlyingToken).safeTransferFrom(_user, address(this), _amount); uint256 received = IERC20(underlyingToken).balanceOf(address(this)) - balBefore; _mint(_user, received);
Add documentation or require statements explicitly rejecting fee-on-transfer and rebasing tokens if the protocol chooses not to support them
Add a governance validation step or token compatibility check in whiteListLendingMarket before deploying a LiquidityGauge
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock fee-on-transfer ERC20 with 1% transfer fee. 2. Deploy LendingLedger and whitelist the mock token as a market with _hasGauge=true. 3. User A deposits 1000 tokens via depositUnderlying - gauge receives 990, mints 1000 gauge tokens. 4. User B deposits 1000 tokens - gauge receives 990, mints 1000 gauge tokens. Gauge balance = 1980, totalSupply = 2000. 5. User A calls withdrawUnderlying(1000) - succeeds (1980 >= 1000). Gauge balance = 980. 6. User B calls withdrawUnderlying(1000) - reverts because 980 < 1000. User B has lost access to 20 tokens of principal.
uncertainties: Whether governance would actually whitelist a fee-on-transfer token is unknown - this is a governance operational risk
Whether any planned underlying tokens (cNOTE etc.) have or could acquire fee-on-transfer behavior through upgrades is unknown
The severity depends entirely on the fee percentage and cumulative deposit volume
additional_testing_needed: Test with a mock fee-on-transfer token to verify the exact deficit accumulation
Test that withdrawUnderlying reverts when gauge is undercollateralized
Test interaction with LendingLedger reward accounting when gauge is undercollateralized
Verify whether any planned underlying tokens are upgradeable proxies that could introduce fees
